1	# Storyboard: dotprod (S3, schema v2). The per-beat contract for the M6
2	# round-trip testbed session, "the dot product as similarity". Authored and
3	# locked 2026-07-04 as the M6 seed (the production graph consumes this; the
4	# attention session's S3 machinery produced its equivalent at M5).
5	# Narration here matches script.md exactly; the script adds the [visual:]
6	# cue timeline per beat. Timing budgeted at ~150 spoken wpm.
7	schemaVersion: 2
8	session: dotprod
9	aha: "The dot product is a similarity meter."
10	openingQuestion: "How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?"
11	closingRetrievalPrompt: >-
12	  Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations
13	  turn them into the score?
14	breadcrumbQuestion: >-
15	  What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?
16	extensionsMenuRef: extensions.yaml
17	notationSchedule:
18	  - symbol: a . b
19	    referent: the multiply-and-add score
20	    firstManipulatedBeat: beat-02
21	    firstSymbolicBeat: beat-03
22	beats:
23	  - id: beat-01
24	    title: The score behind the song
25	    narration: >-
26	      Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have
27	      never heard of. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got
28	      compared, and the comparison said: same taste. So here is the question
29	      of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?
30	      Hold that question. One answer to it runs half of modern machine
31	      learning.
32	    visual: >-
33	      [visual: connect-to-reality] Two columns of numbers slide in side by
34	      side, one for the listener's taste and one for the song; paired
35	      entries light up in periwinkle and a single lilac score readout counts
36	      up as the comparison lands. The question card holds over the frozen
37	      score, half the frame deliberately empty.
38	    tracks: [video, page]
39	    newIdea: >-
40	      Two lists of numbers can be compared into one score, and that one
41	      comparison runs recommendation.
42	    novelElements:
43	      - the two number lists
44	      - the match score readout
45	    onScreenText:
46	      - "How do you score a match?"
47	    colors:
48	      lists: data.observed
49	      score: data.params
50	      background: bg
51	      labels: text
52	    estimatedSeconds: 26
53	  - id: beat-02
54	    title: Turn the probe and feel it
55	    narration: >-
56	      Strip it down. A taste is an arrow: each direction is a quality, and
57	      the length is how hard the song leans that way. Take one probe arrow
58	      and four candidate arrows. Multiply them entry by entry and add. That
59	      single sum is the score. Before you drag anything, call it: which
60	      candidate ends up on top, and does any score go negative? Now turn the
61	      probe and watch the bars answer. Point at a candidate and its score
62	      climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the
63	      same, right? Watch again. The bars moved while the candidates never
64	      moved at all. The score reads agreement of direction, not sameness.
65	    visual: >-
66	      [visual: covary | dynamic-concept] One amber probe arrow sweeps
67	      through directions; four periwinkle candidate arrows hold still; under
68	      each candidate a lilac score bar with a numeric readout climbs and
69	      dies as the probe turns toward and away from it. A freeze frame before
70	      the sweep carries the prediction card; after the sweep the probe parks
71	      and the four scores land as integers on the bars.
72	    tracks: [video, page]
73	    newIdea: >-
74	      Multiply pairwise and add: the score moves continuously with the
75	      probe's direction, and agreement is not identity.
76	    novelElements:
77	      - the probe arrow
78	      - the candidate arrows
79	      - the score bars
80	    onScreenText:
81	      - "probe"
82	      - "candidates"
83	      - "3  2  0  -2"
84	    colors:
85	      probe: data.heat
86	      candidates: data.observed
87	      scores: data.params
88	      background: bg
89	      labels: text
90	    interactiveSpec: page/interactives/dot-alignment.yaml
91	    predictionPrompt:
92	      prompt: >-
93	        Before you drag anything, call it: which candidate ends up on top,
94	        and does any score go negative?

# Storyboard: dotprod (S3, schema v2). The per-beat contract for the M6
# round-trip testbed session, "the dot product as similarity". Authored and
# locked 2026-07-04 as the M6 seed (the production graph consumes this; the
# attention session's S3 machinery produced its equivalent at M5).
# Narration here matches script.md exactly; the script adds the [visual:]
# cue timeline per beat. Timing budgeted at ~150 spoken wpm.
schemaVersion: 2
session: dotprod
aha: "The dot product is a similarity meter."
openingQuestion: "How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?"
closingRetrievalPrompt: >-
  Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations
  turn them into the score?
breadcrumbQuestion: >-
  What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?
extensionsMenuRef: extensions.yaml
notationSchedule:
  - symbol: a . b
    referent: the multiply-and-add score
    firstManipulatedBeat: beat-02
    firstSymbolicBeat: beat-03
beats:
  - id: beat-01
    title: The score behind the song
    narration: >-
      Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have
      never heard of. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got
      compared, and the comparison said: same taste. So here is the question
      of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?
      Hold that question. One answer to it runs half of modern machine
      learning.
    visual: >-
      [visual: connect-to-reality] Two columns of numbers slide in side by
      side, one for the listener's taste and one for the song; paired
      entries light up in periwinkle and a single lilac score readout counts
      up as the comparison lands. The question card holds over the frozen
      score, half the frame deliberately empty.
    tracks: [video, page]
    newIdea: >-
      Two lists of numbers can be compared into one score, and that one
      comparison runs recommendation.
    novelElements:
      - the two number lists
      - the match score readout
    onScreenText:
      - "How do you score a match?"
    colors:
      lists: data.observed
      score: data.params
      background: bg
      labels: text
    estimatedSeconds: 26
  - id: beat-02
    title: Turn the probe and feel it
    narration: >-
      Strip it down. A taste is an arrow: each direction is a quality, and
      the length is how hard the song leans that way. Take one probe arrow
      and four candidate arrows. Multiply them entry by entry and add. That
      single sum is the score. Before you drag anything, call it: which
      candidate ends up on top, and does any score go negative? Now turn the
      probe and watch the bars answer. Point at a candidate and its score
      climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the
      same, right? Watch again. The bars moved while the candidates never
      moved at all. The score reads agreement of direction, not sameness.
    visual: >-
      [visual: covary | dynamic-concept] One amber probe arrow sweeps
      through directions; four periwinkle candidate arrows hold still; under
      each candidate a lilac score bar with a numeric readout climbs and
      dies as the probe turns toward and away from it. A freeze frame before
      the sweep carries the prediction card; after the sweep the probe parks
      and the four scores land as integers on the bars.
    tracks: [video, page]
    newIdea: >-
      Multiply pairwise and add: the score moves continuously with the
      probe's direction, and agreement is not identity.
    novelElements:
      - the probe arrow
      - the candidate arrows
      - the score bars
    onScreenText:
      - "probe"
      - "candidates"
      - "3  2  0  -2"
    colors:
      probe: data.heat
      candidates: data.observed
      scores: data.params
      background: bg
      labels: text
    interactiveSpec: page/interactives/dot-alignment.yaml
    predictionPrompt:
      prompt: >-
        Before you drag anything, call it: which candidate ends up on top,
        and does any score go negative?
      expectedWrongAnswer: >-
        The candidate that looks most similar to the probe wins, and
        nothing goes negative because similarity cannot be less than zero.
    misconceptionBeat: true
    misconception: similarity-means-identical
    estimatedSeconds: 42
  - id: beat-03
    title: Name the machine you just used
    narration: >-
      Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a
      candidate and the sum climbs. Point it across and the sum dies to
      zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. One multiply and
      add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the
      dial. So say it plainly: the dot product is a similarity meter. Two
      lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. And
      the reading tracks direction, because direction is all the sweep ever
      changed.
    visual: >-
      [visual: connect | symbol-sense] The three probe positions replay as
      stills: along, across, against, each with its frozen score. The stills
      collapse into one lilac meter dial whose needle swings from oppose
      through ignore to agree as the probe sweep replays. The aha sentence
      trues up on screen, freehand to exact with a mint glow. A small
      periwinkle mono annotation, a . b, sits on the score readout.
    tracks: [video, page]
    newIdea: >-
      The multiply-and-add score behaves like a meter for alignment: agree
      positive, across zero, oppose negative.
    novelElements:
      - the meter dial
      - the annotation a . b
    onScreenText:
      - "a . b"
      - "agree  ignore  oppose"
      - "The dot product is a similarity meter."
    colors:
      probe: data.heat
      candidates: data.observed
      meter: data.params
      ahaTrueUp: data.fit
      background: bg
      labels: text
    majorReveal: true
    estimatedSeconds: 36
  - id: beat-04
    title: What the meter gets wrong
    narration: >-
      Time to close the loop. Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go
      in, so what two operations turn them into the score? While you think,
      one detour worth naming. Swap the songs for a job posting. Score
      yourself against what the role wants: multiply the matched entries,
      add them up, one number for the fit. Different aisle, same meter. The
      multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant, and that is
      exactly why it travels. Now the honest look. Stretch a candidate
      longer and its score climbs, even though its direction never moved.
      Long songs would win on sheer loudness, and padded resumes would win
      on sheer length. For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that
      keeps the direction and forgets the size? Still up? The menu below
      has three ways to keep going.
    visual: >-
      [visual: dynamic-process | connect-to-reality] The retrieval prompt
      holds as a card while the meter idles. The song columns swap for two
      labeled "you" and "the role": Python hours, SQL reps, nights free;
      matched entries multiply and pool into the same lilac readout on the
      same dial. Then one periwinkle candidate stretches longer; its lilac
      score climbs while its direction holds still; a small rose flag marks
      the unearned win. The walk-home question lands as the closing card,
      and a quiet chip points to the extensions menu on the page.
    tracks: [video, page]
    newIdea: >-
      The raw score rewards length as well as direction, and that gap is
      the next session's opening.
    novelElements:
      - the job-fit columns
      - the stretched candidate
      - the unearned-win flag
    onScreenText:
      - "two arrows in, one number out"
      - "different aisle, same meter"
      - "keep the direction, forget the size"
      - "Still up?"
    colors:
      candidates: data.observed
      score: data.params
      failureFlag: data.error
      background: bg
      labels: text
    estimatedSeconds: 52

file.content:
# How does your app know two songs share your taste?

## The score behind the song

Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. No human picked it for you. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.

That's the whole magic trick, and it's smaller than it looks. One list stood for you. One list stood for the song. Something turned the pair into a single number, and that number was high enough to hit play.

So here is the question of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?

Hold that question before we reach for any definition. One answer to it runs half of modern machine learning. We're going to build that answer with our hands, not memorize it.

## What does a taste look like as numbers?

Strip it down until it fits in your palm. A taste is an arrow. Each direction is a quality, some axis like "loud" or "mellow," and the length along that direction is how hard the song leans that way. Two dimensions, so we can actually see the thing.

Now picture one amber probe arrow, that's you, and four candidate arrows, four songs sitting still. Here's the rule for scoring a match: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add up the products. That single sum is the score. One multiply, one add.

> Honest edges: the numbers in this session are chosen for readability. Real embedding coordinates are neither small nor round. We use tidy integers so your eyes can do the arithmetic the machine does silently.

Before you read on, call it. Which candidate ends up with the highest score, and does any score go negative? Say an answer out loud. A prediction you can be wrong about is worth ten definitions you nod along to.

The tempting guess is that the candidate that looks most like the probe wins, and that no score dips below zero, because how could similarity be less than nothing?

Now turn the probe and watch the scores answer. Point at a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the *same*, right?

Watch again, more carefully. The scores moved while the candidates never moved at all. Nothing about the songs changed. Only your direction changed. So the score can't be reading sameness of items. It's reading agreement of direction.

And yes, scores go negative. Point against a candidate and the sum drops below zero, because some of those pairwise products come out negative and drag the sum down. Similarity, it turns out, has a basement. Before you scroll on, call one more reading: what would a score of exactly zero mean for two songs? Not opposed, not aligned. Say it in your own words, then check yourself against the sweep.

The number measures which way two arrows point, not what the things are.

## What is the machine you just used?

Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs to its biggest. Point it across, at a right angle, and the sum dies to exactly zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative.

Three positions, three readings: agree, ignore, oppose. One multiply and one add, and it behaves like a meter with a needle that swings across those three words on a dial.

So say it plainly:

**The dot product is a similarity meter.**

Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. That's the machine, and it has a symbol now: we write it $a \cdot b$, the dot between the two arrows standing for multiply-pairwise-and-add.

> Honest edges: real embeddings live in hundreds of dimensions. Two dimensions is the picture, not the territory. Nothing in the mechanism changes when you add axes: you still multiply entry by entry and add, there are just more entries. The dial in your head stays exactly as true with hundreds of axes as it is with two.

And notice why the reading tracks direction and not size or identity. Direction is the only thing the sweep ever changed. The meter answers the question you actually moved. Close the loop yourself before the next section: the meter has three readings worth knowing, positive, zero, and negative. State what each one says about two vectors, out loud or on paper, in your own words.

## Where does the meter travel, and where does it break?

Time to close the loop. Do this from memory, not from scrolling up.

Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?

If "multiply pairwise, then add" came back without a peek, you own $a \cdot b$ now. It's yours.

While that settles, one detour worth naming. Swap the songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what the role wants: Python hours, SQL reps, nights free. Multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit. Different aisle, same meter. The multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant, and that's exactly why it travels.

Now one more honest look, because a meter you trust blindly is a meter that will lie to you. Take one candidate and stretch it longer without turning it at all. Its direction holds dead still. Its score climbs anyway.

That's a problem. It means a long, loud song can win on sheer size, not on agreement, and a padded resume can win on sheer length. The meter can't tell "points the same way" apart from "just bigger." A shout beats a whisper even when the whisper agrees with you more. That gap is exactly where the next session opens.

For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?

Still up? The menu below has three ways to keep going: argue the metaphor with an LLM until it cracks, chase this same score into the attention papers where it reappears, or build the meter yourself in a few lines of NumPy.

input.content:
{
  "nbformat": 4,
  "nbformat_minor": 5,
  "metadata": {
    "kernelspec": {
      "display_name": "Python 3",
      "language": "python",
      "name": "python3"
    },
    "language_info": {
      "name": "python",
      "pygments_lexer": "ipython3"
    }
  },
  "cells": [
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "# How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you've never heard of. No human picked it. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.\n",
        "\n",
        "One list stood for you. One list stood for the song. Something turned the pair into a single number, and that number was high enough to hit play.\n",
        "\n",
        "Hold that question before we reach for any definition: how do you score a match between two lists of numbers? We're going to build the answer with code, not memorize it."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {},
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "import numpy as np\n",
        "\n",
        "rng = np.random.default_rng(0)\n",
        "\n",
        "you = np.array([4, 1])    # how much you lean toward each quality\n",
        "song = np.array([3, 2])   # how much the song leans the same way\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"you  :\", you)\n",
        "print(\"song :\", song)"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "A taste is an arrow. Each entry is a quality, like \"energetic\" or \"mellow\", and the number along it is how hard something leans that way.\n",
        "\n",
        "Below is one probe arrow (that's you) and four candidate arrows (four songs). The rule for scoring a match: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add up the products. One multiply, one add, one score.\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run the next cell: call it. Which candidate ends up with the highest score against the probe? Does any score go negative?"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {},
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "candidates = {\n",
        "    \"song_bright\": np.array([4, 3]),\n",
        "    \"song_mellow\": np.array([-3, 4]),\n",
        "    \"song_moody\": np.array([-4, -3]),\n",
        "    \"song_offbeat\": np.array([1, -1]),\n",
        "}\n",
        "probe = np.array([4, 3])\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"probe:\", probe)\n",
        "for name, vec in candidates.items():\n",
        "    print(f\"{name:14s}: {vec}\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {},
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "def dot_score(a, b):\n",
        "    \"\"\"Multiply entry by entry, then add. That's the whole rule.\"\"\"\n",
        "    total = 0.0\n",
        "    for x, y in zip(a, b):\n",
        "        total += x * y\n",
        "    return total\n",
        "\n",
        "scores = {name: dot_score(probe, vec) for name, vec in candidates.items()}\n",
        "\n",
        "for name, score in sorted(scores.items(), key=lambda kv: kv[1], reverse=True):\n",
        "    print(f\"{name:14s}: {score:+.1f}\")\n",
        "\n",
        "# cross-check the hand-rolled version against numpy's own dot\n",
        "for name, vec in candidates.items():\n",
        "    assert dot_score(probe, vec) == np.dot(probe, vec)\n",
        "\n",
        "winner = max(scores, key=scores.get)\n",
        "assert winner == \"song_bright\"\n",
        "assert any(s < 0 for s in scores.values())"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "song_bright wins, because it points the same way as the probe. song_moody goes negative.\n",
        "\n",
        "Now turn the probe and watch the scores answer. Point at a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the *same*, right?\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run the next cell: song_bright never moves below. Only the probe turns. Predict whether its score can still swing from high, to zero, to negative."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {},
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "target = candidates[\"song_bright\"]  # this candidate never changes below\n",
        "\n",
        "for theta in np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 9):\n",
        "    turning_probe = 5 * np.array([np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta)])\n",
        "    score = dot_score(turning_probe, target)\n",
        "    print(f\"theta={theta:4.2f} rad   probe={np.round(turning_probe, 2)}   score={score:+.2f}\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "The candidate never moved. Only the probe's direction changed, and the score swung from strongly positive to zero to negative anyway. The score isn't reading whether two songs are the same. It's reading whether they agree in direction.\n",
        "\n",
        "Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs to its biggest. Point it across, at a right angle, and the sum dies to exactly zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. One multiply-and-add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the dial.\n",
        "\n",
        "**The dot product is a similarity meter.**\n",
        "\n",

      ]
    },

        "target_angle = np.arctan2(target[1], target[0])\n",
        "\n",
        "probe_along = 5 * np.array([np.cos(target_angle), np.sin(target_angle)])\n",
        "probe_across = 5 * np.array([np.cos(target_angle + np.pi / 2), np.sin(target_angle + np.pi / 2)])\n",
        "probe_against = 5 * np.array([np.cos(target_angle + np.pi), np.sin(target_angle + np.pi)])\n",
        "\n",
        "agree = dot_score(probe_along, target)\n",
        "ignore = dot_score(probe_across, target)\n",
        "oppose = dot_score(probe_against, target)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(f\"agree  (along)  : {agree:+.2f}\")\n",
        "print(f\"ignore (across) : {ignore:+.2f}\")\n",
        "print(f\"oppose (against): {oppose:+.2f}\")\n",
        "\n",
        "# the meter reading: positive means agree, ~zero means ignore, negative means oppose\n",
        "assert agree > 0\n",
        "assert np.isclose(ignore, 0, atol=1e-9)\n",
        "assert oppose < 0\n",
        "assert np.isclose(agree, -oppose)  # opposite direction, mirror reading"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?\n",
        "\n",
        "While that settles, one detour worth naming. Swap the songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what the role wants: Python hours, SQL reps, nights free. Multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit. Different aisle, same meter."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {},
      "outputs": [],

        "role_wants = np.array([25, 15, 5])      # what the posting asks for\n",
        "other_candidate = np.array([2, 40, 0])  # heavy sql, no python, no free nights\n",
        "\n",
        "your_fit = dot_score(you_profile, role_wants)\n",
        "other_fit = dot_score(other_candidate, role_wants)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(f\"your fit  : {your_fit:.1f}\")\n",

      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},

        "\n",
        "Now the honest look. Take one candidate and stretch it longer, without turning it at all. Its direction holds dead still.\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run the next cell: predict what happens to its score against the probe. Does stretching change the direction? Does it change the score?"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {},
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "def unit(v):\n",
        "    return v / np.linalg.norm(v)\n",
        "\n",
        "stretched_song = candidates[\"song_bright\"] * 3  # same direction, three times the length\n",
        "\n",
        "assert np.allclose(unit(stretched_song), unit(candidates[\"song_bright\"]))\n",
        "\n",
        "original_score = dot_score(probe, candidates[\"song_bright\"])\n",
        "stretched_score = dot_score(probe, stretched_song)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(f\"original direction : {unit(candidates['song_bright']).round(3)}\")\n",
        "print(f\"stretched direction: {unit(stretched_song).round(3)}\")\n",
        "print(f\"original score     : {original_score:+.2f}\")\n",
        "print(f\"stretched score    : {stretched_score:+.2f}\")\n",
        "\n",
        "assert stretched_score > original_score"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "A long, loud song wins on sheer size, not on agreement. A padded resume wins on sheer length. The meter can't yet tell \"points the same way\" apart from \"just bigger.\"\n",
        "\n",
        "For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?\n",
        "\n",
        "One thing to try next: write a function that divides a vector by its own length before scoring it, so stretching a candidate no longer changes its reading. Confirm with an assert that the new score is the same for `song_bright` and its stretched copy."
      ]
    }
  ]
}

<cell id="cell-0"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type># How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?</cell id="cell-0">
<cell id="cell-1"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.

This notebook builds that comparison from scratch, in NumPy, and proves what it actually measures.</cell id="cell-1">
<cell id="cell-2">import numpy as np

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
print("numpy ready")</cell id="cell-2">
<cell id="cell-3"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>## A taste is an arrow

Strip it down. A taste is an arrow: each direction is a quality, and the length is how hard the song leans that way. Take one probe arrow (that's you) and four candidate arrows (four songs).</cell id="cell-3">
<cell id="cell-4">probe = np.array([1.0, 3.0])
candidates = {
    "song_a": np.array([2.0, 6.0]),
    "song_b": np.array([3.0, -1.0]),
    "song_c": np.array([-2.0, -6.0]),
    "song_d": np.array([0.0, 2.0]),
}

print("probe:", probe)
for name, vec in candidates.items():
    print(name, vec)</cell id="cell-4">
<cell id="cell-5"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>Before you run this: the rule for scoring a match is multiply entry by entry, then add. Call it now, which candidate ends up on top, and does any score go negative?</cell id="cell-5">
<cell id="cell-6">def dot_by_hand(a, b):
    total = 0.0
    for x, y in zip(a, b):
        total += x * y
    return total

scores = {name: dot_by_hand(probe, vec) for name, vec in candidates.items()}
for name, s in scores.items():
    print(name, s)

for name, vec in candidates.items():
    assert abs(dot_by_hand(probe, vec) - np.dot(probe, vec)) < 1e-9</cell id="cell-6">
<cell id="cell-7"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>song_a comes out on top with the highest score. song_b lands at exactly zero. song_c goes negative. Multiply-and-add can drop below zero without hesitation. Similarity has a basement.</cell id="cell-7">
<cell id="cell-8"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>## Turn the probe and feel it

Before you run this: the candidates never move. Only the probe's direction changes. Predict: do the scores change anyway, even though nothing about the songs changed?</cell id="cell-8">
<cell id="cell-9">def rotate(v, theta):
    c, s = np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta)
    return np.array([c * v[0] - s * v[1], s * v[0] + c * v[1]])

probe_turned = rotate(probe, np.pi / 2)
scores_turned = {name: dot_by_hand(probe_turned, vec) for name, vec in candidates.items()}

print("probe was:", probe, "-> now:", probe_turned)
for name in candidates:
    print(name, "before:", scores[name], "after:", scores_turned[name])

assert any(abs(scores[name] - scores_turned[name]) > 1e-6 for name in candidates)</cell id="cell-9">
<cell id="cell-10"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>The candidates never moved. Only the probe turned. The scores answered anyway, so the score can't be reading what the songs *are*. It's reading how the two arrows agree in direction.</cell id="cell-10">
<cell id="cell-11"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>## Agreement is not identity

Before you run this: predict whether a candidate that is not identical to the probe can still score positive, and how a stretched copy of the probe compares to a genuinely different arrow.</cell id="cell-11">
<cell id="cell-12">same_direction = probe * 4  # four times longer, same direction, definitely not identical
different_vector = np.array([3.0, 1.0])  # a genuinely different arrow

score_scaled = dot_by_hand(probe, same_direction)
score_different = dot_by_hand(probe, different_vector)

print("probe . (4x probe) =", score_scaled)
print("probe . (different arrow) =", score_different)

assert not np.array_equal(probe, same_direction)
assert score_scaled > 0
assert score_scaled > score_different</cell id="cell-12">
<cell id="cell-13"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>## Name the machine you just used

Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs. Point it across and the sum dies to zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. One multiply and add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the dial.

**The dot product is a similarity meter.**

Before you run this: predict the sign of each of the three readings below.</cell id="cell-13">
<cell id="cell-14">a = np.array([3.0, 4.0])
along = a * 2                    # same direction, different length
across = np.array([-4.0, 3.0])   # perpendicular to a
against = -a                     # opposite direction

reading_along = dot_by_hand(a, along)
reading_across = dot_by_hand(a, across)
reading_against = dot_by_hand(a, against)

print("along:", reading_along)
print("across:", reading_across)
print("against:", reading_against)

assert reading_along > 0
assert abs(reading_across) < 1e-9
assert reading_against < 0</cell id="cell-14">
<cell id="cell-15"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>Three positions, three readings: agree, ignore, oppose. The dial only ever swings because of direction. That's the whole machine, and it has a symbol: a . b, multiply pairwise and add.</cell id="cell-15">
<cell id="cell-16"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>## Different aisle, same meter

Swap the songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what the role wants: multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit. Before you run this: predict whether the same multiply-and-add can score a job match just as well as a song match.</cell id="cell-16">
<cell id="cell-17"># entries: [python_hours, sql_reps, nights_free]
you = np.array([30.0, 10.0, 2.0])
role_wants = np.array([25.0, 15.0, 1.0])

fit_score = dot_by_hand(you, role_wants)
print("job fit score:", fit_score)

assert fit_score == np.dot(you, role_wants)</cell id="cell-17">
<cell id="cell-18"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>Same operation, different aisle. Multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant. That's exactly why it travels.

## The honest look

Before you run this: predict what happens to the score when a candidate's direction never changes, but it gets stretched to be five times longer.</cell id="cell-18">
<cell id="cell-19">def unit(v):
    return v / np.linalg.norm(v)

candidate_short = np.array([1.0, 1.0])
candidate_long = candidate_short * 5.0

score_short = dot_by_hand(a, candidate_short)
score_long = dot_by_hand(a, candidate_long)

print("direction short:", unit(candidate_short))
print("direction long:", unit(candidate_long))
print("score short:", score_short)
print("score long:", score_long)

assert np.allclose(unit(candidate_short), unit(candidate_long))
assert score_long > score_short</cell id="cell-19">
<cell id="cell-20"><cell_type>markdown</cell_type>The direction never moved, the printout above proves it: the two unit vectors match. The score climbed anyway, just because the arrow got longer. A long, loud song would win on sheer size, not on agreement. A padded resume would win on sheer length.

## For the walk home

What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?

Try this next: take `candidate_long` above, divide it by its own length before scoring it against `a`. See what number you get, then stretch it even further and check whether the score still climbs.</cell id="cell-20">

cells.source: # How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?

Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.

This notebook builds that comparison from scratch, in NumPy, and proves what it actually measures.

import numpy as np

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
print("numpy ready")

## A taste is an arrow

Strip it down. A taste is an arrow: each direction is a quality, and the length is how hard the song leans that way. Take one probe arrow (that's you) and four candidate arrows (four songs).
Before you run this: the rule for scoring a match is multiply entry by entry, then add. Call it now, which candidate ends up on top, and does any score go negative?
song_a comes out on top with the highest score. song_b lands at exactly zero. song_c goes negative. Multiply-and-add can drop below zero without hesitation. Similarity has a basement.

## Turn the probe and feel it

Before you run this: the candidates never move. Only the probe's direction changes. Predict: do the scores change anyway, even though nothing about the songs changed?
The candidates never moved. Only the probe turned. The scores answered anyway, so the score can't be reading what the songs *are*. It's reading how the two arrows agree in direction.

## Agreement is not identity

Before you run this: predict whether a candidate that is not identical to the probe can still score positive, and how a stretched copy of the probe compares to a genuinely different arrow.

same_direction = probe * 4  # four times longer, same direction, definitely not identical
different_vector = np.array([3.0, 1.0])  # a genuinely different arrow

score_scaled = dot_by_hand(probe, same_direction)
score_different = dot_by_hand(probe, different_vector)

print("probe . (4x probe) =", score_scaled)
print("probe . (different arrow) =", score_different)

assert not np.array_equal(probe, same_direction)
assert score_scaled > 0
assert score_scaled > score_different

## Name the machine you just used

Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs. Point it across and the sum dies to zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. One multiply and add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the dial.

**The dot product is a similarity meter.**

Before you run this: predict the sign of each of the three readings below.

a = np.array([3.0, 4.0])
along = a * 2                    # same direction, different length
across = np.array([-4.0, 3.0])   # perpendicular to a
against = -a                     # opposite direction

reading_along = dot_by_hand(a, along)
reading_across = dot_by_hand(a, across)
reading_against = dot_by_hand(a, against)

print("along:", reading_along)
print("across:", reading_across)
print("against:", reading_against)

assert reading_along > 0
assert abs(reading_across) < 1e-9
assert reading_against < 0
Three positions, three readings: agree, ignore, oppose. The dial only ever swings because of direction. That's the whole machine, and it has a symbol: a . b, multiply pairwise and add.

## Different aisle, same meter

Swap the songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what the role wants: multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit. Before you run this: predict whether the same multiply-and-add can score a job match just as well as a song match.

# entries: [python_hours, sql_reps, nights_free]
you = np.array([30.0, 10.0, 2.0])
role_wants = np.array([25.0, 15.0, 1.0])

fit_score = dot_by_hand(you, role_wants)
print("job fit score:", fit_score)

assert fit_score == np.dot(you, role_wants)

Same operation, different aisle. Multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant. That's exactly why it travels.

## The honest look

Before you run this: predict what happens to the score when a candidate's direction never changes, but it gets stretched to be five times longer.

def unit(v):
    return v / np.linalg.norm(v)

candidate_short = np.array([1.0, 1.0])
candidate_long = candidate_short * 5.0

score_short = dot_by_hand(a, candidate_short)
score_long = dot_by_hand(a, candidate_long)

print("direction short:", unit(candidate_short))
print("direction long:", unit(candidate_long))
print("score short:", score_short)
print("score long:", score_long)

assert np.allclose(unit(candidate_short), unit(candidate_long))
assert score_long > score_short

The direction never moved, the printout above proves it: the two unit vectors match. The score climbed anyway, just because the arrow got longer. A long, loud song would win on sheer size, not on agreement. A padded resume would win on sheer length.

## For the walk home

What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?

Try this next: take `candidate_long` above, divide it by its own length before scoring it against `a`. See what number you get, then stretch it even further and check whether the score still climbs.

        "other_candidate = np.array([2, 40, 0])  # heavy sql, no python, no free nights\n",
        "\n",
        "your_fit = dot_score(you_profile, role_wants)\n",
        "other_fit = dot_score(other_candidate, role_wants)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(f\"your fit  : {your_fit:.1f}\")\n",
        "print(f\"other fit : {other_fit:.1f}\")\n",
        "\n",
        "assert your_fit > other_fit"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Same meter, same math, no idea what \"python hours\" or \"nights free\" even mean. That's exactly why it travels.\n",
        "\n",
        "Now the honest look. Take one candidate and stretch it longer, without turning it at all. Its direction holds dead still.\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run the next cell: predict what happens to its score against the probe. Does stretching change the direction? Does it change the score?"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {},
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "def unit(v):\n",
        "    return v / np.linalg.norm(v)\n",
        "\n",
        "stretched_song = candidates[\"song_bright\"] * 3  # same direction, three times the length\n",
        "\n",
        "assert np.allclose(unit(stretched_song), unit(candidates[\"song_bright\"]))\n",
        "\n",
        "original_score = dot_score(probe, candidates[\"song_bright\"])\n",
        "stretched_score = dot_score(probe, stretched_song)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(f\"original direction : {unit(candidates['song_bright']).round(3)}\")\n",
        "print(f\"stretched direction: {unit(stretched_song).round(3)}\")\n",
        "print(f\"original score     : {original_score:+.2f}\")\n",
        "print(f\"stretched score    : {stretched_score:+.2f}\")\n",
        "\n",
        "assert stretched_score > original_score"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "A long, loud song wins on sheer size, not on agreement. A padded resume wins on sheer length. The meter can't yet tell \"points the same way\" apart from \"just bigger.\"\n",
        "\n",
        "For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?\n",
        "\n",
        "One thing to try next: write a function that divides a vector by its own length before scoring it, so stretching a candidate no longer changes its reading. Confirm with an assert that the new score is the same for `song_bright` and its stretched copy."
      ]
    }
  ]
}

  "cells": [

        "# How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you've never heard of. No human picked it. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.\n",
        "\n",
        "One list stood for you. One list stood for the song. Something turned the pair into a single number, and that number was high enough to hit play.\n",
        "\n",
        "Hold that question before we reach for any definition: how do you score a match between two lists of numbers? We're going to build the answer with code, not memorize it."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {},
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "import numpy as np\n",
        "\n",
        "rng = np.random.default_rng(0)\n",
        "\n",
        "you = np.array([4, 1])    # how much you lean toward each quality\n",
        "song = np.array([3, 2])   # how much the song leans the same way\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"you  :\", you)\n",
        "print(\"song :\", song)"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "A taste is an arrow. Each entry is a quality, like \"energetic\" or \"mellow\", and the number along it is how hard something leans that way.\n",
        "\n",
        "Below is one probe arrow (that's you) and four candidate arrows (four songs). The rule for scoring a match: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add up the products. One multiply, one add, one score.\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run the next cell: call it. Which candidate ends up with the highest score against the probe? Does any score go negative?"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {},
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "candidates = {\n",
        "    \"song_bright\": np.array([4, 3]),\n",
        "    \"song_mellow\": np.array([-3, 4]),\n",
        "    \"song_moody\": np.array([-4, -3]),\n",
        "    \"song_offbeat\": np.array([1, -1]),\n",
        "}\n",
        "probe = np.array([4, 3])\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"probe:\", probe)\n",
        "for name, vec in candidates.items():\n",
        "    print(f\"{name:14s}: {vec}\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {},
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "def dot_score(a, b):\n",
        "    \"\"\"Multiply entry by entry, then add. That's the whole rule.\"\"\"\n",
        "    total = 0.0\n",
        "    for x, y in zip(a, b):\n",
        "        total += x * y\n",
        "    return total\n",
        "\n",
        "scores = {name: dot_score(probe, vec) for name, vec in candidates.items()}\n",
        "\n",
        "for name, score in sorted(scores.items(), key=lambda kv: kv[1], reverse=True):\n",
        "    print(f\"{name:14s}: {score:+.1f}\")\n",
        "\n",
        "# cross-check the hand-rolled version against numpy's own dot\n",
        "for name, vec in candidates.items():\n",
        "    assert dot_score(probe, vec) == np.dot(probe, vec)\n",
        "\n",
        "winner = max(scores, key=scores.get)\n",
        "assert winner == \"song_bright\"\n",
        "assert any(s < 0 for s in scores.values())"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "song_bright wins, because it points the same way as the probe. song_moody goes negative.\n",
        "\n",
        "Now turn the probe and watch the scores answer. Point at a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the *same*, right?\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run the next cell: song_bright never moves below. Only the probe turns. Predict whether its score can still swing from high, to zero, to negative."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {},
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "target = candidates[\"song_bright\"]  # this candidate never changes below\n",
        "\n",
        "for theta in np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 9):\n",
        "    turning_probe = 5 * np.array([np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta)])\n",
        "    score = dot_score(turning_probe, target)\n",
        "    print(f\"theta={theta:4.2f} rad   probe={np.round(turning_probe, 2)}   score={score:+.2f}\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "The candidate never moved. Only the probe's direction changed, and the score swung from strongly positive to zero to negative anyway. The score isn't reading whether two songs are the same. It's reading whether they agree in direction.\n",
        "\n",
        "Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs to its biggest. Point it across, at a right angle, and the sum dies to exactly zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. One multiply-and-add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the dial.\n",
        "\n",
        "**The dot product is a similarity meter.**\n",
        "\n",
        "Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. It has a symbol now: $a \\cdot b$, multiply-pairwise-and-add."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {},
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "target_angle = np.arctan2(target[1], target[0])\n",
        "\n",
        "probe_along = 5 * np.array([np.cos(target_angle), np.sin(target_angle)])\n",
        "probe_across = 5 * np.array([np.cos(target_angle + np.pi / 2), np.sin(target_angle + np.pi / 2)])\n",
        "probe_against = 5 * np.array([np.cos(target_angle + np.pi), np.sin(target_angle + np.pi)])\n",
        "\n",
        "agree = dot_score(probe_along, target)\n",
        "ignore = dot_score(probe_across, target)\n",
        "oppose = dot_score(probe_against, target)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(f\"agree  (along)  : {agree:+.2f}\")\n",
        "print(f\"ignore (across) : {ignore:+.2f}\")\n",
        "print(f\"oppose (against): {oppose:+.2f}\")\n",
        "\n",
        "# the meter reading: positive means agree, ~zero means ignore, negative means oppose\n",
        "assert agree > 0\n",
        "assert np.isclose(ignore, 0, atol=1e-9)\n",
        "assert oppose < 0\n",
        "assert np.isclose(agree, -oppose)  # opposite direction, mirror reading"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?\n",
        "\n",
        "While that settles, one detour worth naming. Swap the songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what the role wants: Python hours, SQL reps, nights free. Multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit. Different aisle, same meter."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {},
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "you_profile = np.array([30, 10, 4])     # python hours, sql reps, nights free\n",
        "role_wants = np.array([25, 15, 5])      # what the posting asks for\n",
        "other_candidate = np.array([2, 40, 0])  # heavy sql, no python, no free nights\n",
        "\n",
        "your_fit = dot_score(you_profile, role_wants)\n",
        "other_fit = dot_score(other_candidate, role_wants)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(f\"your fit  : {your_fit:.1f}\")\n",
        "print(f\"other fit : {other_fit:.1f}\")\n",
        "\n",
        "assert your_fit > other_fit"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Same meter, same math, no idea what \"python hours\" or \"nights free\" even mean. That's exactly why it travels.\n",
        "\n",
        "Now the honest look. Take one candidate and stretch it longer, without turning it at all. Its direction holds dead still.\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run the next cell: predict what happens to its score against the probe. Does stretching change the direction? Does it change the score?"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {},
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "def unit(v):\n",
        "    return v / np.linalg.norm(v)\n",
        "\n",
        "stretched_song = candidates[\"song_bright\"] * 3  # same direction, three times the length\n",
        "\n",
        "assert np.allclose(unit(stretched_song), unit(candidates[\"song_bright\"]))\n",
        "\n",
        "original_score = dot_score(probe, candidates[\"song_bright\"])\n",
        "stretched_score = dot_score(probe, stretched_song)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(f\"original direction : {unit(candidates['song_bright']).round(3)}\")\n",
        "print(f\"stretched direction: {unit(stretched_song).round(3)}\")\n",
        "print(f\"original score     : {original_score:+.2f}\")\n",
        "print(f\"stretched score    : {stretched_score:+.2f}\")\n",
        "\n",
        "assert stretched_score > original_score"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "A long, loud song wins on sheer size, not on agreement. A padded resume wins on sheer length. The meter can't yet tell \"points the same way\" apart from \"just bigger.\"\n",
        "\n",
        "For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?\n",
        "\n",
        "One thing to try next: write a function that divides a vector by its own length before scoring it, so stretching a candidate no longer changes its reading. Confirm with an assert that the new score is the same for `song_bright` and its stretched copy."
      ]
    }
  ]
}

        "Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.\n",

        "This notebook builds that comparison from scratch, in NumPy, and proves what it actually measures."
      ]

    {
      "cell_type": "code",

      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "import numpy as np\n",
        "\n",
        "rng = np.random.default_rng(0)\n",
        "print(\"numpy ready\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## A taste is an arrow\n",
        "\n",
        "Strip it down. A taste is an arrow: each direction is a quality, and the length is how hard the song leans that way. Take one probe arrow (that's you) and four candidate arrows (four songs)."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "probe = np.array([1.0, 3.0])\n",
        "candidates = {\n",
        "    \"song_a\": np.array([2.0, 6.0]),\n",
        "    \"song_b\": np.array([3.0, -1.0]),\n",
        "    \"song_c\": np.array([-2.0, -6.0]),\n",
        "    \"song_d\": np.array([0.0, 2.0]),\n",
        "}\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"probe:\", probe)\n",
        "for name, vec in candidates.items():\n",
        "    print(name, vec)"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Before you run this: the rule for scoring a match is multiply entry by entry, then add. Call it now, which candidate ends up on top, and does any score go negative?"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "def dot_by_hand(a, b):\n",
        "    total = 0.0\n",
        "    for x, y in zip(a, b):\n",
        "        total += x * y\n",
        "    return total\n",
        "\n",
        "scores = {name: dot_by_hand(probe, vec) for name, vec in candidates.items()}\n",
        "for name, s in scores.items():\n",
        "    print(name, s)\n",
        "\n",
        "for name, vec in candidates.items():\n",
        "    assert abs(dot_by_hand(probe, vec) - np.dot(probe, vec)) < 1e-9"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "song_a comes out on top with the highest score. song_b lands at exactly zero. song_c goes negative. Multiply-and-add can drop below zero without hesitation. Similarity has a basement."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## Turn the probe and feel it\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run this: the candidates never move. Only the probe's direction changes. Predict: do the scores change anyway, even though nothing about the songs changed?"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "def rotate(v, theta):\n",
        "    c, s = np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta)\n",
        "    return np.array([c * v[0] - s * v[1], s * v[0] + c * v[1]])\n",
        "\n",
        "probe_turned = rotate(probe, np.pi / 2)\n",
        "scores_turned = {name: dot_by_hand(probe_turned, vec) for name, vec in candidates.items()}\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"probe was:\", probe, \"-> now:\", probe_turned)\n",
        "for name in candidates:\n",
        "    print(name, \"before:\", scores[name], \"after:\", scores_turned[name])\n",
        "\n",
        "assert any(abs(scores[name] - scores_turned[name]) > 1e-6 for name in candidates)"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "The candidates never moved. Only the probe turned. The scores answered anyway, so the score can't be reading what the songs *are*. It's reading how the two arrows agree in direction."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## Agreement is not identity\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run this: predict whether a candidate that is not identical to the probe can still score positive, and how a stretched copy of the probe compares to a genuinely different arrow."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "same_direction = probe * 4  # four times longer, same direction, definitely not identical\n",
        "different_vector = np.array([3.0, 1.0])  # a genuinely different arrow\n",
        "\n",
        "score_scaled = dot_by_hand(probe, same_direction)\n",
        "score_different = dot_by_hand(probe, different_vector)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"probe . (4x probe) =\", score_scaled)\n",
        "print(\"probe . (different arrow) =\", score_different)\n",
        "\n",
        "assert not np.array_equal(probe, same_direction)\n",
        "assert score_scaled > 0\n",
        "assert score_scaled > score_different"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## Name the machine you just used\n",
        "\n",
        "Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs. Point it across and the sum dies to zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. One multiply and add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the dial.\n",
        "\n",
        "**The dot product is a similarity meter.**\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run this: predict the sign of each of the three readings below."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "a = np.array([3.0, 4.0])\n",
        "along = a * 2                    # same direction, different length\n",
        "across = np.array([-4.0, 3.0])   # perpendicular to a\n",
        "against = -a                     # opposite direction\n",
        "\n",
        "reading_along = dot_by_hand(a, along)\n",
        "reading_across = dot_by_hand(a, across)\n",
        "reading_against = dot_by_hand(a, against)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"along:\", reading_along)\n",
        "print(\"across:\", reading_across)\n",
        "print(\"against:\", reading_against)\n",
        "\n",
        "assert reading_along > 0\n",
        "assert abs(reading_across) < 1e-9\n",
        "assert reading_against < 0"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Three positions, three readings: agree, ignore, oppose. The dial only ever swings because of direction. That's the whole machine, and it has a symbol: a . b, multiply pairwise and add."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## Different aisle, same meter\n",
        "\n",
        "Swap the songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what the role wants: multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit. Before you run this: predict whether the same multiply-and-add can score a job match just as well as a song match."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "# entries: [python_hours, sql_reps, nights_free]\n",
        "you = np.array([30.0, 10.0, 2.0])\n",
        "role_wants = np.array([25.0, 15.0, 1.0])\n",
        "\n",
        "fit_score = dot_by_hand(you, role_wants)\n",
        "print(\"job fit score:\", fit_score)\n",
        "\n",
        "assert fit_score == np.dot(you, role_wants)"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "Same operation, different aisle. Multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant. That's exactly why it travels.\n",
        "\n",
        "## The honest look\n",
        "\n",
        "Before you run this: predict what happens to the score when a candidate's direction never changes, but it gets stretched to be five times longer."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "metadata": {},
      "execution_count": null,
      "outputs": [],
      "source": [
        "def unit(v):\n",
        "    return v / np.linalg.norm(v)\n",
        "\n",
        "candidate_short = np.array([1.0, 1.0])\n",
        "candidate_long = candidate_short * 5.0\n",
        "\n",
        "score_short = dot_by_hand(a, candidate_short)\n",
        "score_long = dot_by_hand(a, candidate_long)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"direction short:\", unit(candidate_short))\n",
        "print(\"direction long:\", unit(candidate_long))\n",
        "print(\"score short:\", score_short)\n",
        "print(\"score long:\", score_long)\n",
        "\n",
        "assert np.allclose(unit(candidate_short), unit(candidate_long))\n",
        "assert score_long > score_short"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "The direction never moved, the printout above proves it: the two unit vectors match. The score climbed anyway, just because the arrow got longer. A long, loud song would win on sheer size, not on agreement. A padded resume would win on sheer length.\n",
        "\n",
        "## For the walk home\n",
        "\n",
        "What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?\n",
        "\n",
        "Try this next: take `candidate_long` above, divide it by its own length before scoring it against `a`. See what number you get, then stretch it even further and check whether the score still climbs."
      ]
    }
  ]
}

# Storyboard: dotprod (S3, schema v2). The per-beat contract for the M6
# round-trip testbed session, "the dot product as similarity". Authored and
# locked 2026-07-04 as the M6 seed (the production graph consumes this; the
# attention session's S3 machinery produced its equivalent at M5).
# Narration here matches script.md exactly; the script adds the [visual:]
# cue timeline per beat. Timing budgeted at ~150 spoken wpm.
schemaVersion: 2
session: dotprod
aha: "The dot product is a similarity meter."
openingQuestion: "How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?"
closingRetrievalPrompt: >-
  Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations
  turn them into the score?
breadcrumbQuestion: >-
  What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?
extensionsMenuRef: extensions.yaml
notationSchedule:
  - symbol: a . b
    referent: the multiply-and-add score
    firstManipulatedBeat: beat-02
    firstSymbolicBeat: beat-03
beats:
  - id: beat-01
    title: The score behind the song
    narration: >-
      Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have
      never heard of. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got
      compared, and the comparison said: same taste. So here is the question
      of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?
      Hold that question. One answer to it runs half of modern machine
      learning.
    visual: >-
      [visual: connect-to-reality] Two columns of numbers slide in side by
      side, one for the listener's taste and one for the song; paired
      entries light up in periwinkle and a single lilac score readout counts
      up as the comparison lands. The question card holds over the frozen
      score, half the frame deliberately empty.
    tracks: [video, page]
    newIdea: >-
      Two lists of numbers can be compared into one score, and that one
      comparison runs recommendation.
    novelElements:
      - the two number lists
      - the match score readout
    onScreenText:
      - "How do you score a match?"
    colors:
      lists: data.observed
      score: data.params
      background: bg
      labels: text
    estimatedSeconds: 26
  - id: beat-02
    title: Turn the probe and feel it
    narration: "Strip it down. A taste is an arrow: each direction is a quality,
      and the length is how hard the song leans that way. Take one probe arrow
      and four candidate arrows. Multiply them entry by entry and add. That
      single sum is the score. Before you drag anything, call it: which
      candidate ends up on top, and does any score go negative? Now turn the
      probe and watch the bars answer. Point at a candidate and its score
      climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the
      same, right? Watch again. The bars moved while the candidates never moved
      at all. The score reads agreement of direction, not sameness."
    visual: "[visual: covary | dynamic-concept] One amber probe arrow sweeps
      through directions; four periwinkle candidate arrows hold still; under
      each candidate a lilac score bar with a numeric readout climbs and dies as
      the probe turns toward and away from it. Each candidate label sits nudged
      outward past its arrow tip so the sweeping amber probe never buries it,
      staying readable at every probe angle including when the probe passes
      candidate B. A freeze frame before the sweep carries the prediction card;
      after the sweep the probe parks and the four scores land as integers on
      the bars."
    tracks:
      - video
      - page
    newIdea: "Multiply pairwise and add: the score moves continuously with the
      probe's direction, and agreement is not identity."
    novelElements:
      - the probe arrow
      - the candidate arrows
      - the score bars
    onScreenText:
      - probe
      - candidates
      - 3  2  0  -2
    colors:
      probe: data.heat
      candidates: data.observed
      scores: data.params
      background: bg
      labels: text
    interactiveSpec: page/interactives/dot-alignment.yaml
    predictionPrompt:
      prompt: "Before you drag anything, call it: which candidate ends up on top,
        and does any score go negative?"
      expectedWrongAnswer: The candidate that looks most similar to the probe wins,
        and nothing goes negative because similarity cannot be less than zero.
    majorReveal: false
    misconceptionBeat: true
    misconception: similarity-means-identical
    estimatedSeconds: 42
  - id: beat-03
    title: Name the machine you just used
    narration: >-
      Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a
      candidate and the sum climbs. Point it across and the sum dies to
      zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. One multiply and
      add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the
      dial. So say it plainly: the dot product is a similarity meter. Two
      lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. And
      the reading tracks direction, because direction is all the sweep ever
      changed.
    visual: >-
      [visual: connect | symbol-sense] The three probe positions replay as
      stills: along, across, against, each with its frozen score. The stills
      collapse into one lilac meter dial whose needle swings from oppose
      through ignore to agree as the probe sweep replays. The aha sentence
      trues up on screen, freehand to exact with a mint glow. A small
      periwinkle mono annotation, a . b, sits on the score readout.
    tracks: [video, page]
    newIdea: >-
      The multiply-and-add score behaves like a meter for alignment: agree
      positive, across zero, oppose negative.
    novelElements:
      - the meter dial
      - the annotation a . b
    onScreenText:
      - "a . b"
      - "agree  ignore  oppose"
      - "The dot product is a similarity meter."
    colors:
      probe: data.heat
      candidates: data.observed
      meter: data.params
      ahaTrueUp: data.fit
      background: bg
      labels: text
    majorReveal: true
    estimatedSeconds: 36
  - id: beat-04
    title: What the meter gets wrong
    narration: >-
      Time to close the loop. Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go
      in, so what two operations turn them into the score? While you think,
      one detour worth naming. Swap the songs for a job posting. Score
      yourself against what the role wants: multiply the matched entries,
      add them up, one number for the fit. Different aisle, same meter. The
      multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant, and that is
      exactly why it travels. Now the honest look. Stretch a candidate
      longer and its score climbs, even though its direction never moved.
      Long songs would win on sheer loudness, and padded resumes would win
      on sheer length. For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that
      keeps the direction and forgets the size? Still up? The menu below
      has three ways to keep going.
    visual: >-
      [visual: dynamic-process | connect-to-reality] The retrieval prompt
      holds as a card while the meter idles. The song columns swap for two
      labeled "you" and "the role": Python hours, SQL reps, nights free;
      matched entries multiply and pool into the same lilac readout on the
      same dial. Then one periwinkle candidate stretches longer; its lilac
      score climbs while its direction holds still; a small rose flag marks
      the unearned win. The walk-home question lands as the closing card,
      and a quiet chip points to the extensions menu on the page.
    tracks: [video, page]
    newIdea: >-
      The raw score rewards length as well as direction, and that gap is
      the next session's opening.
    novelElements:
      - the job-fit columns
      - the stretched candidate
      - the unearned-win flag
    onScreenText:
      - "two arrows in, one number out"
      - "different aisle, same meter"
      - "keep the direction, forget the size"
      - "Still up?"
    colors:
      candidates: data.observed
      score: data.params
      failureFlag: data.error
      background: bg
      labels: text
    estimatedSeconds: 52

content.content:
1	# How does your app know two songs share your taste?
2	
3	## The score behind the song
4	
5	Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. No human picked it for you. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.
6	
7	That's the whole magic trick, and it's smaller than it looks. One list stood for you. One list stood for the song. Something turned the pair into a single number, and that number was high enough to hit play.
8	
9	So here is the question of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?
10	
11	Hold that question before we reach for any definition. One answer to it runs half of modern machine learning. We're going to build that answer with our hands, not memorize it.
12	
13	Before you read on, take a guess: if you had to turn two lists of numbers into a single match score, what would you do to them?
14	
15	## What does a taste look like as numbers?
16	
17	Strip it down until it fits in your palm. A taste is an arrow. Each direction is a quality, some axis like "loud" or "mellow," and the length along that direction is how hard the song leans that way. Two dimensions, so we can actually see the thing.
18	
19	Now picture one amber probe arrow, that's you, and four candidate arrows, four songs sitting still. Here's the rule for scoring a match: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add up the products. That single sum is the score. One multiply, one add.
20	
21	> Honest edges: the numbers in this session are chosen for readability. Real embedding coordinates are neither small nor round. We use tidy integers so your eyes can do the arithmetic the machine does silently.
22	
23	Before you read on, call it. Which candidate ends up with the highest score, and does any score go negative? Say an answer out loud. A prediction you can be wrong about is worth ten definitions you nod along to.
24	
25	The tempting guess is that the candidate that looks most like the probe wins, and that no score dips below zero, because how could similarity be less than nothing?
26	
27	Now turn the probe and watch the scores answer. Point at a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the *same*, right?
28	
29	Watch again, more carefully. The scores moved while the candidates never moved at all. Nothing about the songs changed. Only your direction changed. So the score can't be reading sameness of items. It's reading agreement of direction.
30	
31	And yes, scores go negative. Point against a candidate and the sum drops below zero, because some of those pairwise products come out negative and drag the sum down. Similarity, it turns out, has a basement.
32	
33	The number measures which way two arrows point, not what the things are.
34	
35	Before you scroll on, call one more reading: what would a score of exactly zero mean for two songs? Not opposed, not aligned. Say it in your own words, then check yourself against the sweep.
36	
37	## What is the machine you just used?
38	
39	Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs to its biggest. Point it across, at a right angle, and the sum dies to exactly zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative.
40	
41	Three positions, three readings: agree, ignore, oppose. One multiply and one add, and it behaves like a meter with a needle that swings across those three words on a dial.
42	
43	So say it plainly:
44	
45	**The dot product is a similarity meter.**
46	
47	Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. That's the machine, and it has a symbol now: we write it $a \cdot b$, the dot between the two arrows standing for multiply-pairwise-and-add.
48	
49	> Honest edges: real embeddings live in hundreds of dimensions. Two dimensions is the picture, not the territory. Nothing in the mechanism changes when you add axes: you still multiply entry by entry and add, there are just more entries. The dial in your head stays exactly as true with hundreds of axes as it is with two.
50	
51	And notice why the reading tracks direction and not size or identity. Direction is the only thing the sweep ever changed. The meter answers the question you actually moved. Close the loop yourself before the next section: the meter has three readings worth knowing, positive, zero, and negative.
